name: Codebase
question: How is your application's source code managed?
hint: One codebase tracked in version control, many deploys.

options.text: Single repo, deployed to multiple environments (dev/staging/prod)
Multiple repos for the same logical app
One repo, but only one environment
No version control used

name: Dependencies
question: How do you handle your app's dependencies?
hint: Dependencies must be explicitly declared and isolated.

options.text: Declared in a manifest (package.json, requirements.txt, etc.) and installed in an isolated env
Declared but not isolated (system-level packages)
Assumed to be pre-installed on the server
Dependencies are bundled directly in the repo

name: Config
question: Where does your app store configuration (DB URLs, API keys, feature flags)?
hint: Config must be stored in environment variables, not code.

options.text: In environment variables, injected at runtime
In config files checked into version control
Hardcoded directly in the source code
Mix of env vars and config files (not in VCS)

name: Backing services
question: How does your app interact with databases, queues, or external APIs?
hint: Treat backing services as attached resources accessible via URL.

options.text: Via URLs/credentials in env vars - easy to swap providers without code changes
Connection details are in code but abstracted via config files
Directly hardcoded, tightly coupled to specific providers
Services are co-located on the same host, not via network

name: Build, release, run
question: How are your build, release, and run stages handled?
hint: Strictly separate build -> release -> run stages.

options.text: CI/CD pipeline: build artifact -> combine with config -> deploy immutable release
Build and deploy are separate but config is baked into the build
Code is pushed directly to production and compiled/installed there
No formal pipeline - manual steps each time

name: Processes
question: How do your app processes handle state?

options.text: Stateless - sessions/data stored in external services (Redis, DB, S3, etc.)
Mostly stateless, but sticky sessions are used for some flows
State stored in-memory or on local disk between requests
Single long-running process that holds all state

name: Port binding
question: How does your app expose its services?
hint: The app is self-contained and exports services via port binding.

options.text: App binds to a port and serves requests itself (e.g. via Express, Gunicorn)
App relies on an external server (Apache mod_php, IIS) injecting it
App is deployed as a serverless function - no persistent port
Not sure how the app is served

name: Concurrency
question: How do you scale your app when load increases?
hint: Scale out by adding more processes, not by making one process bigger.

options.text: Horizontal scaling - spin up more stateless process instances
Vertical scaling only - upgrade server hardware/memory
Threads within a single process to handle concurrency
No scaling strategy - single instance always

name: Disposability
question: How does your app handle startup and shutdown?
hint: Maximize robustness with fast startup and graceful shutdown.

options.text: Fast startup, handles SIGTERM gracefully, finishes in-flight requests
Startup is quick but shutdown is abrupt (kills connections)
Startup takes minutes; no graceful shutdown
No attention has been given to startup/shutdown behavior

name: Dev/prod parity
question: How similar are your development and production environments?
hint: Keep dev, staging, and prod as similar as possible.

options.text: Near-identical - same OS, services, and versions (Docker, containers)
Similar services but different versions or OS
Dev uses mocks/stubs; prod uses real services
Dev runs on local machine with no resemblance to prod

name: Logs
question: How does your app handle logs?
hint: Treat logs as event streams - do not manage log files yourself.

options.text: App writes to stdout/stderr; infra routes to a log aggregator (Datadog, ELK, etc.)
App writes log files that are shipped externally by an agent
App manages log files and rotation itself
No structured logging - print statements or nothing

name: Admin processes
question: How do you run one-off admin tasks (migrations, scripts, data fixes)?
hint: Run admin tasks as one-off processes in the same environment.

options.text: Same codebase, same env - run via a one-off command (e.g. heroku run, kubectl exec)
Separate scripts run manually on a server with SSH
Admin tasks are triggered via API endpoints in the app
No standard process - ad hoc each time
